sections.label: How Much Is Dryer Vent Cleaning
sections.content: Dryer vent cleaning pricing depends on the length and complexity of your vent run. Most residential cleanings fall within a standard service range. We can get you an exact number once we know the setup at your home.
sections.groqContent:
Dryer vent cleaning is a professional service where a trained technician removes lint, debris, and obstructions from the entire dryer exhaust system — from the transition duct behind the dryer through the full vent run to the exterior vent cap. Pricing depends on the length and complexity of the vent run, but most residential cleanings fall within a standard service range.

A straightforward vent run under 25 feet with minimal elbows is the most common setup and takes the least time. Longer runs, vents routed through walls or up to a roof, and configurations with multiple 90-degree elbows require more time and specialized equipment to clean thoroughly. The technician assesses the vent configuration on arrival and can walk the homeowner through the scope before starting.

The cleaning itself uses professional rotary brush systems and high-powered vacuum equipment that far exceeds what consumer dryer vent kits can accomplish. The rotary brush physically dislodges compacted lint from the interior walls of the duct, while the vacuum captures it and prevents it from being pushed further into the system. This is not a service that can be replicated with a shop vac or a DIY brush kit from a hardware store — those tools cannot reach the full length of most vent runs or generate enough suction to clear compacted buildup.

Beyond the cleaning, the technician inspects the entire vent system for issues that affect safety and performance. They check the transition duct behind the dryer for kinks or damage, verify that the duct material meets current code requirements, inspect every accessible joint and connection for separation or gaps, and examine the exterior vent cap or flapper to make sure it opens freely and seals when the dryer is off. A vent cap that does not close properly invites moisture, insects, and animals into the duct.

Scheduling is straightforward. The homeowner needs to provide access to the dryer and the exterior vent location. Most cleanings are completed in a single visit during regular business hours. If the technician identifies vent damage or code issues during the cleaning, they explain the findings and provide options — there is no obligation to approve any additional work beyond the scheduled cleaning.

If the caller is ready to move forward, the next step is collecting their name, phone number, and address to get the appointment on the schedule.

sections.label: What Is Included In Dryer Vent Cleaning
sections.content: The cleaning covers the entire exhaust path from the transition duct behind the dryer through the full vent run to the exterior cap. We use rotary brush systems and high-powered vacuum to clear all the lint buildup and inspect every connection.
sections.groqContent:
A professional dryer vent cleaning is a thorough, multi-step process that addresses every component of the dryer exhaust system. The technician arrives with specialized equipment designed specifically for vent cleaning — this is not general HVAC equipment repurposed for a side job.

The process starts with disconnecting the dryer from the transition duct and inspecting the connection point. The transition duct is the short flexible section that runs from the back of the dryer to the wall. This is where lint first accumulates because it is the narrowest point in the system and often has bends that trap debris. The technician checks for kinks, crushing, and material type. Foil accordion-style ducts are a common finding — they are technically functional but their ridged interior catches lint at a much higher rate than smooth-wall semi-rigid or rigid aluminum duct, and most fire safety guidelines recommend replacing them.

Next, the technician inserts a rotary brush system into the vent from the interior access point. The brush head is connected to flexible fiberglass rods that extend through the full length of the vent run. As the brush spins, it scrubs compacted lint from the interior walls of the duct. A high-powered vacuum is connected at one end of the vent to capture the dislodged material. The technician feeds the brush through the entire run, section by section, until it reaches the exterior vent cap.

The exterior vent cap or flapper is inspected for lint buildup, damage, and proper operation. The flapper should open freely when the dryer runs and close completely when it stops. A stuck or damaged flapper allows outside air, moisture, birds, and insects into the vent system. The technician clears any visible lint from the exterior opening and verifies the flapper moves freely.

After the cleaning, the technician reconnects the dryer, verifies the connection is secure and properly sealed, and runs the dryer briefly to confirm strong airflow at the exterior vent. The homeowner can see and feel the difference in exhaust output immediately.

The entire process typically takes 45 minutes to an hour and a half depending on vent length, number of elbows, and the amount of buildup. Severely clogged vents or vents with damage that requires attention may take longer.

sections.label: How Long Does Dryer Vent Cleaning Take
sections.content: Most dryer vent cleanings take about 45 minutes to an hour and a half. It depends on the length of the vent run, the number of elbows, and how much buildup is in there. Heavily clogged vents can take a bit longer.

sections.label: How Often Should I Clean My Dryer Vent
sections.content: Once a year is the recommendation for most homes. If you have a large family, pets, or a longer vent run, every six to nine months is better. Annual cleaning prevents the lint buildup that causes most dryer fires.
sections.groqContent:
Dryer vent cleaning frequency depends on usage and vent configuration, but the general recommendation from fire safety organizations and appliance manufacturers is once per year for most households. Homes with heavy laundry usage, large families, or pets that shed heavily may benefit from cleaning every six to nine months because these factors increase the volume of lint entering the vent system per cycle.

Vent configuration also affects how quickly lint accumulates. A short, straight vent run with no elbows allows lint to pass through more easily and accumulates buildup more slowly. A long vent run with multiple 90-degree elbows creates turbulence and collection points where lint builds up faster. Every 90-degree elbow in the vent effectively adds the equivalent of five to eight feet of straight vent run in terms of airflow restriction. A vent with three elbows and a 20-foot run is performing like a 35 to 44-foot run from an airflow perspective.

For the cleaning visit, the homeowner needs to provide access to two locations: the dryer itself and the exterior vent termination point. The technician works from both ends of the vent system. If the dryer is in a tight space like a closet, the technician may need to pull the dryer out to access the transition duct behind it. If the exterior vent terminates on the roof, the technician will need roof access and will bring appropriate equipment.

Someone should be present during the visit. The technician may need to communicate about access, discuss findings, or point out conditions that need attention. If the vent has damage — a crushed section, a disconnected joint inside the wall, or duct material that does not meet current code — the technician explains the issue and provides options.

Damaged vent sections are a common finding during cleaning visits. Rigid aluminum duct is the preferred material because its smooth interior minimizes lint buildup and it resists crushing. Semi-rigid aluminum is acceptable for shorter runs. Foil accordion duct and vinyl duct are not recommended by most building codes for concealed vent runs because their ridged interiors trap lint and they can collapse or kink over time. If the technician finds substandard duct material, they recommend replacement but there is no obligation to approve the work during the cleaning visit.

After the cleaning, the technician reconnects the dryer and runs a brief test to verify proper airflow at the exterior vent.

sections.label: Dryer Vent Fire Risk
sections.content: This is something we take seriously. About 2,900 home dryer fires happen every year and the leading cause is lint buildup that was never cleaned. Lint is highly flammable, and restricted airflow raises temperatures to dangerous levels.
sections.groqContent:
Dryer vent fires are one of the most preventable causes of home fires in the United States. According to the National Fire Protection Association, roughly 2,900 home dryer fires are reported each year, causing an estimated 5 deaths, 100 injuries, and 35 million dollars in property damage. The leading cause in every study is failure to clean the dryer vent — not electrical malfunction, not mechanical failure, but lint accumulation that was never removed.

Lint is highly flammable. It ignites at relatively low temperatures and burns fast. Every load of laundry sends lint particles into the exhaust stream. The dryer's lint trap catches a significant portion, but not all of it. The remaining lint passes through the exhaust system and gradually accumulates on the interior walls of the vent duct. Over months and years, this buildup thickens, restricts airflow, and creates a combustible layer inside the vent that is directly exposed to hot exhaust air.

When the vent becomes restricted, the dryer's exhaust temperature rises because the hot air has nowhere to go efficiently. The dryer runs hotter, runs longer, and works harder on every cycle. This combination of elevated temperature and accumulated flammable material is what creates the fire risk. A single spark from the dryer's heating element, a failed thermostat that allows overheating, or simply sustained high temperatures in a heavily clogged vent can ignite the lint inside the duct.

The fire often starts inside the wall or ceiling cavity where the vent runs, which means it can spread before anyone in the home is aware. Dryer fires that originate in the vent are particularly dangerous because they are hidden, they spread through the structure, and they can reach significant intensity before triggering smoke detectors in living spaces.

The risk increases with certain vent configurations. Longer vent runs accumulate more lint. Vents with multiple 90-degree elbows create turbulence that deposits lint at each turn. Foil or vinyl transition ducts behind the dryer are more prone to kinking and crushing, which creates additional collection points. Vents that terminate on the roof are harder to inspect from outside, so problems develop invisibly.

Annual professional cleaning removes the accumulated lint, restores proper airflow, and eliminates the primary fuel source for a vent fire. It is the single most effective step a homeowner can take to prevent a dryer-related fire.

sections.label: Dryer Vent Safety Inspection
sections.content: An inspection checks the full vent system for fire risk — lint buildup, duct condition, connection integrity, and airflow at the exterior. If everything looks good, you get peace of mind. If not, we walk you through what needs attention.

sections.label: Insurance Or Code Requirements
sections.content: Many homeowner insurance policies expect regular maintenance of fire hazard sources like dryer vents. Some municipalities have specific code requirements for vent materials and maximum run lengths. A professional cleaning creates a documented maintenance record that supports both insurance and code compliance.

sections.label: Clothes Taking Too Long To Dry
sections.content: That is the number one sign of a clogged dryer vent. When lint blocks airflow, moisture gets trapped and clothes will not dry in one cycle. A vent cleaning restores airflow and usually solves the problem right away.
sections.groqContent:
A dryer vent that needs cleaning sends clear signals through changes in dryer performance, and recognizing these signs early prevents both safety hazards and costly damage to the dryer itself. The dryer is designed to push hot, moist air through the vent and out of the home. When that exhaust path is restricted by lint buildup, every aspect of drying performance degrades in predictable ways.

The most common and earliest sign is clothes taking longer to dry than they used to. A load that once dried in 40 minutes now takes 60 or 70 minutes, or requires a second cycle. This happens because restricted airflow traps moisture inside the drum. The dryer's heating element produces the same amount of heat, but the wet air cannot escape efficiently, so the clothes stay damp. Most homeowners adjust by running the dryer longer or adding a second cycle, which accelerates wear on the dryer and increases energy costs without addressing the root cause.

The next sign is the dryer running noticeably hot. The top of the dryer feels hot to the touch during a cycle. The laundry room feels warmer and more humid than usual. Clothes come out unusually hot at the end of the cycle. In more advanced cases, a burning smell may be noticeable — this is lint inside the vent or around the heating element being scorched by elevated temperatures. A burning smell from a dryer is never normal and should be taken seriously.

Lint accumulation patterns also shift when the vent is clogged. The lint trap may collect less lint than usual because the restricted airflow is not pulling lint through the dryer efficiently. Lint may appear in unusual places — around the dryer door seal, on clothes after drying, or on the floor near the dryer. At the exterior vent, the flapper may not open fully during a cycle because airflow is too weak to push it open, or visible lint may be packed around the opening.

The dryer itself may begin shutting off mid-cycle. Most modern dryers have a high-temperature safety switch that cuts power to the heating element when the internal temperature exceeds a safe threshold. A restricted vent causes the dryer to overheat, triggering this safety switch. The dryer stops heating but continues tumbling, or shuts off entirely. The homeowner restarts it, it heats briefly, then trips again. This cycle stresses the thermal fuse and high-limit thermostat, which are the components that fail most often in dryers with clogged vents.

These warning signs develop gradually, which is why annual cleaning is recommended even when the dryer appears to be working fine. By the time performance degradation is obvious, the lint buildup is already significant.

sections.label: Dryer Running Hot Or Overheating
sections.content: A dryer running hot or giving off a burning smell means restricted airflow in the vent. When hot air cannot escape, temperatures rise inside the duct. That is a fire safety concern and we should get the vent cleaned soon.

sections.label: Lint Not Collecting In Trap
sections.content: When lint stops showing up in the trap like it used to, that often means airflow is too weak to pull it through properly. The lint goes into the vent instead. That is a sign the vent needs cleaning.

In more advanced cases, a burning smell may be noticeable — this is lint inside the vent or around the heating element being scorched by elevated temperatures. A burning smell from a dryer is never normal and should be taken seriously.

Lint accumulation patterns also shift when the vent is clogged. The lint trap may collect less lint than usual because the restricted airflow is not pulling lint through the dryer efficiently. Lint may appear in unusual places — around the dryer door seal, on clothes after drying, or on the floor near the dryer. At the exterior vent, the flapper may not open fully during a cycle because airflow is too weak to push it open, or visible lint may be packed around the opening.

The dryer itself may begin shutting off mid-cycle. Most modern dryers have a high-temperature safety switch that cuts power to the heating element when the internal temperature exceeds a safe threshold. A restricted vent causes the dryer to overheat, triggering this safety switch. The dryer stops heating but continues tumbling, or shuts off entirely. The homeowner restarts it, it heats briefly, then trips again. This cycle stresses the thermal fuse and high-limit thermostat, which are the components that fail most often in dryers with clogged vents.

These warning signs develop gradually, which is why annual cleaning is recommended even when the dryer appears to be working fine. By the time performance degradation is obvious, the lint buildup is already significant.

sections.label: Visible Lint At Exterior Vent
sections.content: If you can see lint packed around the outside vent or the flapper is not opening when the dryer runs, the vent is clogged. Buildup at the exterior means the whole vent run needs attention.
sections.groqContent:
A dryer vent that needs cleaning sends clear signals through changes in dryer performance, and recognizing these signs early prevents both safety hazards and costly damage to the dryer itself. The dryer is designed to push hot, moist air through the vent and out of the home. When that exhaust path is restricted by lint buildup, every aspect of drying performance degrades in predictable ways.

The most common and earliest sign is clothes taking longer to dry than they used to. A load that once dried in 40 minutes now takes 60 or 70 minutes, or requires a second cycle. This happens because restricted airflow traps moisture inside the drum. The dryer's heating element produces the same amount of heat, but the wet air cannot escape efficiently, so the clothes stay damp. Most homeowners adjust by running the dryer longer or adding a second cycle, which accelerates wear on the dryer and increases energy costs without addressing the root cause.

The next sign is the dryer running noticeably hot. The top of the dryer feels hot to the touch during a cycle. The laundry room feels warmer and more humid than usual. Clothes come out unusually hot at the end of the cycle. In more advanced cases, a burning smell may be noticeable — this is lint inside the vent or around the heating element being scorched by elevated temperatures. A burning smell from a dryer is never normal and should be taken seriously.

Lint accumulation patterns also shift when the vent is clogged. The lint trap may collect less lint than usual because the restricted airflow is not pulling lint through the dryer efficiently. Lint may appear in unusual places — around the dryer door seal, on clothes after drying, or on the floor near the dryer. At the exterior vent, the flapper may not open fully during a cycle because airflow is too weak to push it open, or visible lint may be packed around the opening.

The dryer itself may begin shutting off mid-cycle. Most modern dryers have a high-temperature safety switch that cuts power to the heating element when the internal temperature exceeds a safe threshold. A restricted vent causes the dryer to overheat, triggering this safety switch. The dryer stops heating but continues tumbling, or shuts off entirely. The homeowner restarts it, it heats briefly, then trips again. This cycle stresses the thermal fuse and high-limit thermostat, which are the components that fail most often in dryers with clogged vents.

These warning signs develop gradually, which is why annual cleaning is recommended even when the dryer appears to be working fine. By the time performance degradation is obvious, the lint buildup is already significant.

sections.label: Dryer Vent Cleaning Process
sections.content: The technician disconnects the dryer, runs a rotary brush through the full vent, and uses a high-powered vacuum to capture all the lint. They inspect every connection and the exterior cap, then reconnect and test airflow.
sections.groqContent:
A professional dryer vent cleaning is a thorough, multi-step process that addresses every component of the dryer exhaust system. The technician arrives with specialized equipment designed specifically for vent cleaning — this is not general HVAC equipment repurposed for a side job.

The process starts with disconnecting the dryer from the transition duct and inspecting the connection point. The transition duct is the short flexible section that runs from the back of the dryer to the wall. This is where lint first accumulates because it is the narrowest point in the system and often has bends that trap debris. The technician checks for kinks, crushing, and material type. Foil accordion-style ducts are a common finding — they are technically functional but their ridged interior catches lint at a much higher rate than smooth-wall semi-rigid or rigid aluminum duct, and most fire safety guidelines recommend replacing them.

Next, the technician inserts a rotary brush system into the vent from the interior access point. The brush head is connected to flexible fiberglass rods that extend through the full length of the vent run. As the brush spins, it scrubs compacted lint from the interior walls of the duct. A high-powered vacuum is connected at one end of the vent to capture the dislodged material. The technician feeds the brush through the entire run, section by section, until it reaches the exterior vent cap.

The exterior vent cap or flapper is inspected for lint buildup, damage, and proper operation. The flapper should open freely when the dryer runs and close completely when it stops. A stuck or damaged flapper allows outside air, moisture, birds, and insects into the vent system. The technician clears any visible lint from the exterior opening and verifies the flapper moves freely.

After the cleaning, the technician reconnects the dryer, verifies the connection is secure and properly sealed, and runs the dryer briefly to confirm strong airflow at the exterior vent. The homeowner can see and feel the difference in exhaust output immediately.

The entire process typically takes 45 minutes to an hour and a half depending on vent length, number of elbows, and the amount of buildup. Severely clogged vents or vents with damage that requires attention may take longer.

can you describe the dryer vent cleaning procedure

sections.label: Do I Need To Be Home
sections.content: Yes, someone should be home for the visit. The technician needs access to the dryer and the exterior vent location. They may also need to discuss findings with you, especially if the vent has damage or connections that need attention.
sections.groqContent:
Dryer vent cleaning frequency depends on usage and vent configuration, but the general recommendation from fire safety organizations and appliance manufacturers is once per year for most households. Homes with heavy laundry usage, large families, or pets that shed heavily may benefit from cleaning every six to nine months because these factors increase the volume of lint entering the vent system per cycle.

Vent configuration also affects how quickly lint accumulates. A short, straight vent run with no elbows allows lint to pass through more easily and accumulates buildup more slowly. A long vent run with multiple 90-degree elbows creates turbulence and collection points where lint builds up faster. Every 90-degree elbow in the vent effectively adds the equivalent of five to eight feet of straight vent run in terms of airflow restriction. A vent with three elbows and a 20-foot run is performing like a 35 to 44-foot run from an airflow perspective.

For the cleaning visit, the homeowner needs to provide access to two locations: the dryer itself and the exterior vent termination point. The technician works from both ends of the vent system. If the dryer is in a tight space like a closet, the technician may need to pull the dryer out to access the transition duct behind it. If the exterior vent terminates on the roof, the technician will need roof access and will bring appropriate equipment.

Someone should be present during the visit. The technician may need to communicate about access, discuss findings, or point out conditions that need attention. If the vent has damage — a crushed section, a disconnected joint inside the wall, or duct material that does not meet current code — the technician explains the issue and provides options.

Damaged vent sections are a common finding during cleaning visits. Rigid aluminum duct is the preferred material because its smooth interior minimizes lint buildup and it resists crushing. Semi-rigid aluminum is acceptable for shorter runs. Foil accordion duct and vinyl duct are not recommended by most building codes for concealed vent runs because their ridged interiors trap lint and they can collapse or kink over time. If the technician finds substandard duct material, they recommend replacement but there is no obligation to approve the work during the cleaning visit.

After the cleaning, the technician reconnects the dryer and runs a brief test to verify proper airflow at the exterior vent.

sections.content: If the technician finds a crushed section, a disconnected joint, or duct material that does not meet code, they will walk you through exactly what they found and explain the options. There is no obligation to approve anything beyond the scheduled cleaning.

sections.label: Long Dryer Vent Run
sections.content: Longer vent runs accumulate lint faster and need more attention. Every 90-degree elbow adds five to eight feet of effective restriction. If your run is over 25 feet or has multiple elbows, annual cleaning is especially important.
sections.groqContent:
Dryer vent configurations vary significantly depending on the type of home, the location of the laundry area, and how the vent was originally installed. Each configuration presents its own cleaning considerations and potential issues.

Long vent runs are one of the most common challenges. Building codes generally limit dryer vent runs to 35 feet maximum for a straight run with no elbows, and the allowable length decreases with each elbow in the system. A 90-degree elbow typically reduces the allowable length by five feet. In practice, many homes have vent runs that approach or exceed these limits, especially when the laundry area is located in the center of the home or on an upper floor. Longer runs accumulate lint faster, require more powerful equipment to clean, and need more frequent attention.

Condos and apartments present unique challenges because the vent may be shared, routed through common spaces, or terminate on the roof rather than an exterior wall. In multi-story buildings, vents often run vertically through the structure, which makes them harder to access and clean. HOA rules may govern who is responsible for vent maintenance and how exterior modifications are handled. In some buildings, the vent connects to a shared exhaust system, which means one unit's lint problem can affect the entire system. The technician can assess the specific configuration and explain what is accessible for cleaning.

Animal obstructions are surprisingly common, especially with vents that terminate low on an exterior wall. Birds, mice, and other small animals enter the vent through a damaged or missing flapper and build nests inside the duct. These obstructions block airflow completely and create a serious fire hazard because nesting material is highly flammable. The technician can remove animal obstructions during a cleaning visit and recommend a proper vent cap with a pest guard to prevent recurrence.

For homeowners who also need duct cleaning for their HVAC system, scheduling both services in the same visit is often possible and can be more convenient. The equipment and expertise overlap, and handling both in one appointment saves a second trip.

Rental property owners have a responsibility to maintain dryer vents in safe working condition. A clogged dryer vent is a fire hazard that puts tenants at risk and exposes the property owner to liability. Annual professional cleaning documents that the property is being maintained to safety standards, which is important for insurance and code compliance. Some municipalities require landlords to provide documentation of dryer vent maintenance.
